I agree with option D: Needle insertion in 2nd ICS. The patient exhibits classic signs of tension pneumothorax—hypotension, tachypnea, and respiratory distress—all of which indicate imminent hemodynamic failure. According to current guidelines, even though chest X-ray confirms the diagnosis post-intervention, needle decompression is the immediate lifesaving action when suspicion arises in a hemodynamically unstable patient. Delaying needle thoracostomy until imaging could result in death due to unrelieved intrathoracic pressure causing cardiac arrest.
\boxed{D}

Current guidelines emphasize that needle decompression at the second intercostal space mid-clavicular line is the fastest way to relieve intrathoracic pressure and restore perfusion in unstable patients, preceding definitive chest tube placement or imaging.
